The speech she delivered in the **1925** INC session sparked a sense of patriotism among those who attended the session, which was honored by prominent politicians such as Motilal Nehru, Madan Mohan Malaviya, and M.R. Jayakar.
Abdullah Abdur Rehman, the leader of the Indian community in South Africa, also participated in the session with the aim of highlighting the plight of Indians living abroad C. Motilal Nehru-1. Amritsar, **1919**.
Jallianwala Bagh Massacre was condemned strongly in **1919** INC session. The Jallianwala Bagh massacre was a horrific event that took place on April 13, **1919**, in Amritsar, Punjab.
The crowd had gathered at the bagh to protest the arrests of two respected freedom struggle leaders, Dr. Saifuddin Kitchlew and Dr. Satyapal.
British troops on the order of General Dyer opened fire on a peaceful gathering of unarmed Indians, killing hundreds and injuring thousands. D. Dr. Rajendra Prasad-2. Bombay, **1934** Jawaharlal Nehru presented the resolution advocating the establishment of a Constituent Assembly tasked with crafting an appropriate Constitution for an independent India in **1934** session.
Furthermore, during this session, decisions were taken to reshape the structure of the Congress. The number of Congress delegates was limited to **2000**, effectively reducing the strength of the All India Congress Committee (AICC) by half.
**Important Tips** About Sarojini Naidu:
- Known as Bharatiya Kokila (The Nightingale of India).
- Indian independence activist, poet, and politician.
- Born on February 13, 1879, in Hyderabad, India.
- Joined Indian national movement due to Bengal partition in **1905**.
- Received 'Kaisar-i-Hind' Medal for service during plague epidemic.
Contribution to Indian Freedom Movement:
- First Indian Woman President of INC in **1925** (Kanpur Session).
- Participated in Non-Cooperation Movement (**1920**).
- Led Salt Satyagraha in **1930** against British salt monopoly.
- Arrested during "Quit India" movement in **1942**, jailed for 21 months.
- Traveled globally to raise awareness about India's struggle. Political Contributions:
- Accompanied Gandhiji to second Round Table Conference (**1931**).
- Became Governor of Uttar Pradesh post-independence.
- Poetry and Literary Works:
- Renowned poetess in English and Urdu.
- Famous works include "In the Bazaars of Hyderabad", "The Golden Threshold", "The Bird of Time", and "The Broken Wing".
Women's Empowerment:
- Advocate for women's rights and empowerment.
- Member of All-India Women's Conference.

questions.subject: History
statementLines: Aurobindo Ghosh was brilliantly defended in the Alipore Conspiracy Case by-
questions.questionText: Aurobindo Ghosh was brilliantly defended in the Alipore Conspiracy Case by-
questions.explanationMarkdown:
**Explanation:** Option a is the correct answer. Chittaranjan Das appeared as a lawyer for Aurobindo Ghosh in the Alipore conspiracy case. Chittaranjan Das is popularly called 'Deshbandhu'.
The Alipore Bomb Case Trial refers to an attempt to murder the district judge of Muzaffarpur. The Alipore Bomb case conspiracy took place in the year **1908**. It is also known as the Manicktolla bomb conspiracy or Muraripukur conspiracy.
The case saw the trial of a number of Indian nationalists of the Anushilan Samiti in Calcutta, under charges of "Waging war against the Government" of the British Raj.
**Important Tips** Aurobindo Ghosh:
- Aurobindo Ghosh, born on 15 August 1872 in Calcutta district (present-day Kolkata), West Bengal, was an Indian nationalist, poet, philosopher, and yogi.
- He played an active role in the Indian Freedom Movement till **1910**, after which he became a spiritual reformer.
- Ravindra Nath Tagore painted him as the Messiah of Indian Culture and Civilization and C R Das hailed him as the 'poet of patriotism, the prophet of nationalism and the lover of humanity.'
- Aurobindo associated himself with journals and periodicals like the Jugantar, Bande Mataram and the Karmayogi, through which he criticised British imperialism and preached a revolutionary concept of nationalism.
- In **1908**, he was arrested on the charge of the Alipore Bomb Conspiracy Case and was acquitted in **1909** after a long trial.
- Some of his important writings are The Life Divine, Savitri, Essay on the Gita, Defense of Indian Culture, etc.
- He passed away on 5 December **1950**.
Chittaranjan Das:
- He believed in extremist ideology and supported the beliefs of the Lal-Bal-Pal trio.
- He also contributed to the English weekly 'Bande Mataram' along with Aurobindo Ghosh.
- He championed the cause of Khadi and cottage industries and gave up his own western clothes and luxurious lifestyle.
- He, along with Motilal Nehru, established the Swaraj Party in January **1923**.
- He was a prolific writer and poet. He published his collection of poems in two volumes titled 'Malancha' and 'Mala'.

questions.subject: History
statementLines: A provisional Government of India with Raja Mahendra Pratap as its President was established during the first World War in?
questions.questionText: A provisional Government of India with Raja Mahendra Pratap as its President was established during the first World War in?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
**Explanation:** Option a is the correct answer. A provisional government of India was established in Kabul, Afghanistan in December **1915** by a group of Indian revolutionaries, led by Raja Mahendra Pratap.
The government was intended to be a government-in-exile that would overthrow British rule in India. The provisional government was not recognized by any other countries, but it did receive some support from Germany and Turkey.

questions.subject: History
statementLines: Mahatma Gandhi presided over only in one session of the Indian National Congress in
1924. Where was the session held?
questions.questionText: Mahatma Gandhi presided over only in one session of the Indian National Congress in 1924. Where was the session held?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
**Explanation:** Option c is the correct answer. Mahatma Gandhi presided over only one session of the Indian National Congress in **1924**. The session was held in Belgaum, Karnataka.
During his presidential address, Mahatma Gandhi discussed the importance of unity between Hindus and Muslims, the boycott of foreign products, the promotion of spinning and weaving Khadi, efforts to eliminate untouchability, and various other matters.

questions.subject: History
statementLines: Who among the following started the 'Mitra Mela' union?
questions.questionText: Who among the following started the 'Mitra Mela' union?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
**Explanation:** Option b is the correct answer. Mitra Mela was a revolutionary organisation founded by Vinayak Damodar Savarkar along with his brother Ganesh Damodar Savarkar.
They founded the Mitra Mela, a secret organization of revolutionaries, in Nashik in 1899. In **1904**, it was renamed Abhinav Bharat Society.

questions.subject: History
statementLines: Who among of the following led the agitation against the partition of Bengal (1905)?
questions.questionText: Who among of the following led the agitation against the partition of Bengal (1905)?
questions.explanationMarkdown: **Explanation:** Option a is the correct answer. Surendranath Banerjee and Krishna Kumar Mitra led the agitation against the Bengal partition in **1905**. The decision to partition the state of Bengal was announced by Lord Curzon, the then Viceroy of India in July **1905**.

questions.subject: History
statementLines: Who was the first to suggest the boycott of British goods in Bengal?
questions.questionText: Who was the first to suggest the boycott of British goods in Bengal?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
**Explanation:** Option b is the correct answer. Krishna Kumar Mitra was the first to suggest the boycott of British goods in Bengal. The Swadeshi Movement had its genesis in the anti-partition movement which was started to oppose the British decision to partition Bengal.
**Important Tips** Krishna Kumar Mitra:
- Krishna Kumar Mitra (1852
- **1936**) was one of the leaders of the Brahma Samaj and the swadeshi movement.
- In 1883, Krishna Kumar launched a nationalist Bengali weekly, Sanjibani, in cooperation with Kalishankar and others. As editor, he endeavoured to raise the latent nationalistic consciousness of the people-He spread the Swadeshi idea through his paper, 'Sanjeevani'. In one of his issues, he suggested that people should pressurise the British government to repeal the partition by boycotting British goods.

questions.subject: History
statementLines: Who among the following led the Swadeshi Movement at Delhi?
questions.questionText: Who among the following led the Swadeshi Movement at Delhi?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
**Explanation:** Option d is the correct answer. Swadeshi movement started on 7 August **1905**. It started with the partition of Bengal by the Viceroy of India, Lord Curzon in **1905** and continued up to **1911**. It was the most successful of the pre-Gandhian movement.
It was led by Syed Haider Raja in Delhi, Chidambaram Pillai in Madras, Lala Lajpat Rai and Ajit Singh in Punjab and Uttar Pradesh, Lokmanya Tilak in Mumbai and Pune.

questions.subject: History
statementLines: Who among the following leaders was a supporter of 'Swadeshi'?
questions.questionText: Who among the following leaders was a supporter of 'Swadeshi'?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
**Explanation:** Option c is the correct answer. Dadabhai Naoroji, positioned between moderates and extremists, supported swadeshi in a distinct manner.
While not as radical as Tilak, Naoroji endorsed swaraj and swadeshi in his **1906** Calcutta Congress speech. He shared the end goal with extremists but differed on tactics.
Although criticized by both groups, his support for swaraj and the swadeshi movement aligned with extremist sentiments. His endorsement spurred them to pursue evolving ideas beyond moderate strategies.
**Important Tips** Leaders stand on swadeshi movement:
- While Aurobindo initially endorsed passive resistance during the Swadeshi movement, he believed in the ethical validity of armed struggle if circumstances allowed.
He criticized labeling all violent methods as criminal, advocating strategic choices. His focus was on achieving national emancipation above all else, considering liberty paramount.
Aurobindo's approach blended both violence and non-violence as methods for freedom, distancing himself from being an active proponent of the Swadeshi movement's principles.
- Pherozeshah Mehta, despite being associated with the Liberal School of Indian politics, had a more cautious approach towards the Swadeshi movement.
While he supported modernization and education, Mehta was not as fervently aligned with the radical aspects of the movement. His focus was on constitutional means and administrative reform, rather than the more assertive tactics like strikes and boycotts.
Mehta's aversion to violent methods and his desire for gradual, constructive change set him apart from the more extremist proponents of Swadeshi during his time.
- Subhas Chandra Bose, while appreciating the Swadeshi Movement's economic objectives, believed in a more assertive and proactive approach.
He advocated for a direct confrontation with British colonial rule through radical means, emphasizing on aggressive mass mobilization, militant actions, and organized resistance.
Bose aimed for a swift and forceful expulsion of the British rather than gradual economic boycotts, emphasizing the need for more active and assertive strategies to achieve India's independence.

questions.subject: History
statementLines: Yugantar Party was led by-
questions.questionText: Yugantar Party was led by-
questions.explanationMarkdown:
**Explanation:** Option a is the correct answer. Jatin Mukherjee, better known as Bagha Jatin, was an Indian independence activist who was born in 1879 in the division of Bengal's undivided Nadia district.
He was the main figurehead of the Yugantar party, the primary organization of Bengal's revolutionary independence activists. In **1903**, Jatin met Aurobindo Ghose and highly inspired by his teachings. He accepted to join a project of organizing secret society aimed at an armed uprising all over India.
Bhagha Jatin assumed leadership of Yugantar party in **1908**. **Important Tips** Jugantar party:
- The Jugantar party was established by Barindra Kumar Ghosh (Younger brother of Aurobindo Ghosh), Bhupendranath Datta, Raja Subodh Mallik in April **1906**. Barindra Kumar Ghosh and Bagha Jatin were the main leaders.
- Yugantar was one of the two major secret revolutionary movements of Anushilan Samiti fighting for Indian independence in Bengal.
- During the First World War, the Jugantar party arranged to import German arms and ammunition through sympathizers and revolutionaries abroad.
Bagha Jatin asked Rashbehari Bose to take charge of Upper India, aiming to bring about an all-India insurrection in what has come to be called the 'German Plot' or the 'Zimmerman Plan'.

questions.subject: History
statementLines: Who among the following was never elected as a President of the 'Indian National Congress'?
questions.questionText: Who among the following was never elected as a President of the 'Indian National Congress'?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
**Explanation:** Option d is the correct answer. Bal Gangadhar Tilak was never elected president of the Indian National Congress. He was nominated for the presidency several times, but he was always defeated by more moderate leaders. This was because Tilak was seen as too radical by many Congress members.
**Important Tips** Significant Presidents of INC:
- Lala Lajpat Rai was elected president of the Special Session of the Indian National Congress at Kolkata in **1920**.
- Annie Besant was elected president of the Indian National Congress in **1917** and **1933**.
- Moti Lal Nehru was elected president of the Indian National Congress in **1929**.

questions.subject: History
statementLines: The Haripura Session of Indian National Congress, 1938 was presided over by:
questions.questionText: The Haripura Session of Indian National Congress, 1938 was presided over by:
questions.explanationMarkdown: **Explanation:** Option d is the correct answer. The Haripura Session of the Indian National Congress was presided over by Subhash Chandra Bose in **1938**. National Planning Committee was set up under the chairmanship of Jawaharlal Nehru.

questions.subject: History
statementLines: Who founded the 'Anushilan Samiti'?
questions.questionText: Who founded the 'Anushilan Samiti'?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
**Explanation:** Option a is the correct answer. Anushilan Samiti (Calcutta) was founded on 24 March **1902**. Pramathanath Mitra, known widely as P. Mitra, was a Bengali Indian barrister and Indian nationalist who was among the earliest founding members of the Indian revolutionary organisation Anushilan Samiti.
The people associated with this samiti were Sri Aurobindo Ghosh, Chittaranjan Das, Surendranath Tagore, Jatindranath Banerjee, Bagha Jatin, Bhupendra Natha Datta, Barindra Kumar Ghosh etc.

questions.subject: History
statementLines: Who had established "India Independence League"?
questions.questionText: Who had established "India Independence League"?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
**Explanation:** Option c is the correct answer. In **1915**, Rash Behari Bose fled from India to avoid arrest by the British government. He went to Japan and then to Germany. In Germany, he established the Indian Independence League in **1915**. The league was committed to promoting Indian independence through political and diplomatic means.
The Indian Independence League was active in Europe and Asia during World War I. The league also had branches in India. **Important Tips**
- Captain Mohan Singh, an Indian soldier who fought for the British, was captured and held as a prisoner of war by the Japanese forces.
While in captivity, a Japanese army officer persuaded him to collaborate with them for the cause of India's independence. As a result, he was appointed as the leader of the Indian National Army.
- Subhash Chandra Bose joined the ranks of the Indian National Army in **1943**. One of his most renowned pronouncements was, "Tum Mujhe khun do, main tumhe Azadi doonga" (You give me blood, and I will give you freedom).
Under Subhash Bose's guidance, the INA played a pivotal role in the struggle for independence

questions.subject: History
questions.microTopic: Gandhian Phase (Post-1917 till Partition, including NCM, CDM, QIM, Simon Commission, etc.)
statementLines: Who suggested launching a no tax campaign as a protest against the Rowlatt Act?
questions.questionText: Who suggested launching a no tax campaign as a protest against the Rowlatt Act?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
**Explanation:** Option d is the correct answer. Swami Shraddhanand was a great Arya Samajist, social worker, educationalist, and freedom fighter of the country.
He started working with Congress for the liberation of Bharat Mata. He strongly condemned the Jallianwala Bagh Massacre of 13 April **1919** and joined the nationwide protest against the Rowlatt Act in Delhi.
He also suggested launching a no tax campaign as a protest against the Rowlatt Act. Shraddhanand led a procession at Chandni Chowk, Delhi, against the oppressive Rowlatt Act.
**Important Tips** Swami Shraddhanand-He was born on 22 February 1856 at the village Talwan, district Jalandhar of Punjab.
- Swami Shraddhanand's childhood name was Brihaspati but his father Lala Nanak Chand always called him Munshi Ram.
- He began his career as a lawyer and then practiced at Phillaur and Jalandhar.
- He met Swami Dayanand in September 1879 at Bareilly where he visited the place for the promotion of Vedic religion.
- After hearing Dayanand's views on Vedic religion, Shraddhanand's mind completely changed.
He took up the mission to propagate the principles of Arya Samaj and Vedic ideas among the people.
- He also raised his voice for Hindu-Muslim unity.

questions.subject: History
statementLines: Name the organization formed by Surendranath Banerjee which merged with the Indian National Congress in 1886:
questions.questionText: Name the organization formed by Surendranath Banerjee which merged with the Indian National Congress in 1886:
questions.explanationMarkdown:
**Explanation:** Option c is the correct answer. The organization formed by Surendranath Banerjee that merged with the Indian National Congress in 1886 was the Indian Association.
The Indian Association played a significant role in the early Indian nationalist movement, advocating for Indian political rights. It initiated the idea of an all-India conference, leading to the Indian National Conference, and eventually merged with the Indian National Congress in 1886 to work collectively for Indian interests.

questions.subject: History
statementLines: Who among the following transformed the traditional Ganapati festival of Maharashtra into a national festival and gave it a political character?
questions.questionText: Who among the following transformed the traditional Ganapati festival of Maharashtra into a national festival and gave it a political character?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
**Explanation:** Option d is the correct answer. Bal Gangadhar Tilak, a prominent figure in India's freedom struggle, transformed the traditional Ganapati festival into a national event with political significance.
Recognizing the power of unity, Tilak initiated public celebrations in 1893 to foster a sense of togetherness. He introduced large hoardings and immersion of massive Ganesha statues on the tenth day, emphasizing collective participation.
This move played a crucial role in channeling the festival's spirit towards India's independence movement.

questions.subject: History
statementLines: The Indian Home Rule Society in London was started by-
questions.questionText: The Indian Home Rule Society in London was started by-
questions.explanationMarkdown: **Explanation:** Option d is the correct answer. Shyamji Krishna Verma was an Indian freedom fighter and social reformer who founded the Indian Home Rule Society in London in **1905**. The society was a radical organization that advocated for complete independence from British rule.

questions.subject: History
statementLines: Who among the following had founded the 'Indian Society of Oriental Art' to revive ancient art traditions of India?
questions.questionText: Who among the following had founded the 'Indian Society of Oriental Art' to revive ancient art traditions of India?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
**Explanation:** Option a is the correct answer. Abanindranath Tagore established the Indian society of Oriental art along with his brother Gagendranath Tagore in **1907** in Calcutta to revive the ancient art traditions of India.
This Indian society of Oriental art sponsored by Europeans, much popularized Tagore's Bengal School. **Important Tips** Abanindranath Tagore (1871
- **1951**)
- He was the gifted landscape and portrait painter.
- His first painting was Radha Krishna series and his set of paintings called Krishna Lila is the synthesis of the Indian and European style.
- His Arabian Nights series is among his most renowned work.
- Tagore best-known painting is Bharat Mata which depicted Young women portrayed with 4arms in a manner of Hindu deity holding objects.
- He was the principal of government school of art and a great artist of modern India.

questions.subject: History
questions.microTopic: Gandhian Phase (Post-1917 till Partition, including NCM, CDM, QIM, Simon Commission, etc.)
statementLines: Who resigned from the membership of Viceroy's Executive Council as a protest against Jallianwala Bagh Massacre?
questions.questionText: Who resigned from the membership of Viceroy's Executive Council as a protest against Jallianwala Bagh Massacre?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
**Explanation:** Option c is the correct answer. Shankaran Nair became a member of the Viceroy's Executive Council in **1915**. Jalianwala Bagh Massacre took place on April 13, **1919**.
About 20,000 protestors had assembled at bagh to demonstrate against the Rowlatt act. General Dyer open fired at peaceful demonstrators. About 379 people were killed and more than 1000 were injured. Following the Jalianwala Bagh Massacre Shankaran Nair resigned from Viceroy's Council in **1919** as a protest.

questions.explanationMarkdown:
**Explanation:** Option b is the correct answer. A. British Indian Association-3. Radha Kant Dev B. Bombay Presidency Association-2. K.T. Telang C. Central Mohammadan National Association-4. Sayyid Amir Ali D. Servants of India Society-1. Gopal Krishna Gokhale **Important Tips** British Indian Association:
- The British Indian Association, established in 1851 in Kolkata, India, was a political organization advocating for Indian welfare and rights.
Radhakanta Deb served as its first President, and it included members like Peary Chand Mitra. The association demanded Indian education, end to East India Company's monopoly, support for local industry, and Indian civil service inclusion.
Its demands influenced the Indian National Congress. The association ceased in **1954** with the end of the Zamindari system. Central National Muhamedan Association:
- The Central National Muhamedan Association, founded by Syed Ameer Ali in Calcutta in 1877, aimed to uplift Muslims through lawful means, promoting education, moral values, and political consciousness.
It countered Hindu-dominated organizations, seeking Muslim welfare and loyalty to the British government. With branches across India and London, it played a role in modernizing Muslims and addressing their issues. Servants of India Society:
- The Servants of India Society, founded in Pune, Maharashtra, in **1905** by Gopal Krishna Gokhale, aimed to promote social and human development while opposing British rule.
It focused on education, health care, sanitation, and social issues like untouchability and women's rights. Remaining apolitical, it declined after Gokhale's death, with Gandhi's movement gaining prominence. It continues with limited activities today.

questions.subject: History
statementLines: Who among the following was not the member of the Hindustan Republican Association (HRA)?
questions.questionText: Who among the following was not the member of the Hindustan Republican Association (HRA)?
questions.explanationMarkdown: **Explanation:** Option d is the correct answer. Shiv Verma was the member of Hindustan Socialist Republican Association (HSRA), which was founded in **1928**. He was not the member of HRA which was formed in **1924**. Ram Prasad Bismil was the founding member of HRA.In 1924-25**, many young people joined the HRA including Bhagat Singh, Sukhdev and Chandrasekhar Azad.

During the same year and place, he also established the Home Rule League in April **1916**.

statementLines: Given below is a list of organizations. Select those engaged in revolutionary activities, using the codes given below the lists:
1. Abhinav Bharat

questions.questionText: Given below is a list of organizations. Select those engaged in revolutionary activities, using the codes given below the lists: 1. Abhinav Bharat 2. Anushilan Samiti 3. New Nationalist Party 4. Indian Patriot Association Code:
questions.explanationMarkdown:
**Explanation:** Option b is the correct answer. Option 1 is correct: Abhinav Bharat society was founded by Vinayak Damodar Savarkar in **1904**. It was engaged in revolutionary activities.
It was one among several such revolutionary societies functioning in Maharashtra at that time, which believed in the overthrow of British rule through armed rebellion.
Vinayak Savarkar and Ganesh Savarkar founded the revolutionary secret society Mitra Mela in Nasik in 1899. Vinayak Savarkar renamed it Abhinav Bharat in **1904**.
Option 2 is correct: Anushilan Samiti was formed by Satish Chandra Basu in **1902**.He named it after Bankimchandra's play Anushilan-Tattva or theory of discipline and Pramathanath Mitra was its main patron.
The society's goal was to organise and train the youth to start a revolutionary war against the British. Option 3 is incorrect: New Nationalist Party was not any such famous organisation which was engaged in revolutionary activities.
Option 4 is incorrect: Indian Patriotic Association was formed in 1888 by Sir Syed Ahmed Khan and Raja Shiv Prasad Singh of Benaras. The main objective of this association was to create a platform for the Indian Muslims to express their loyalty towards the British Raj and to promote the interest of the Indian Muslims.
It was not engaged in revolutionary activities against British Raj.

questions.subject: History
statementLines: Which of the following pair got sentence to be hanged for assassination of the British officers in England?
questions.questionText: Which of the following pair got sentence to be hanged for assassination of the British officers in England?

options.c: Madan Lal Dhingra and Udham Singh
options.d: Kartar Singh Sarabha and Ashfaqullah Khan

questions.explanationMarkdown:
**Explanation:** Option c is the correct answer. Madan Lal Dhingra was a revolutionary who assassinated Sir Curzon Wyllie, a British political figure, in London in **1909**. He was sentenced to death and hanged.
Udham Singh was a revolutionary who assassinated Michael O'Dwyer, a British official who was responsible for the Jallianwala Bagh massacre, in London in **1940**. He was also sentenced to death and hanged.
**Important Tips**
- Rajguru and Sukhdev were revolutionary associated with Bhagat Singh. They were hanged in India for their involvement in the murder of John Saunders, a British police officer.
- Khudiram Bose and Surya Sen were revolutionary associated with the Anushilan Samiti. They were hanged in India for their involvement in the Muzaffarpur Conspiracy Case.
- Kartar Singh Sarabha and Ashfaqullah Khan were revolutionary associated with the Ghadar Party. They were hanged in India for their involvement in the Lahore Conspiracy Case

questions.subject: History
statementLines: Seeds of discard were in which event during National Movement, and which eventually divided the country, was-
questions.questionText: Seeds of discard were in which event during National Movement, and which eventually divided the country, was-

options.d: Reservation of seats and separate electorates for Muslims in legislative assemblies.

questions.explanationMarkdown:
**Explanation:** Option d is the correct answer. Morley-Minto reforms introduced Reservation of seats and separate electorates for the Muslims.
Some constituencies were earmarked for Muslims and only Muslims could vote their representatives. This event sow the seeds of discard which eventually caused to divide the country

questions.subject: History
statementLines: Who is regarded as the "Mother of Indian Revolution"?
questions.questionText: Who is regarded as the "Mother of Indian Revolution"?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
**Explanation:** Option d is the correct answer. Bhikaiji Rustom Cama, acclaimed as the Mother of the Indian Revolution, played a pivotal role in the overseas Indian freedom struggle.
On August 22, **1907**, she accomplished a historic feat by being the first to unfurl the Indian flag on foreign soil in Stuttgart, Germany. Her powerful advocacy for human rights, equality, and self-governance from British rule resonated through her compelling descriptions of the devastation wrought by a famine in the Indian subcontinent.
She eloquently proclaimed, 'This is the flag of independent India. I appeal to all gentlemen to stand and salute the Flag.' Collaborating with Shyamji Krishna Varma, she designed the altered 'Calcutta flag' which she raised.
Additionally, from Paris, she authored, published, and disseminated revolutionary materials. When the British proscribed 'Vande Mataram', she responded by composing 'Bande Mataram'.
Her unyielding spirit extended to creating 'Madan's Talwar' in response to the execution of Madan Lal Dhingra. **Important Tips** Ramabai Ambedkar:
Ramabai Bhimrao Ambedkar (7 February **1898-27** May **1935**) was the first spouse of B. R. Ambedkar, whose unwavering support played a pivotal role in enabling him to pursue advanced education and realize his full potential.
B. R. Ambedkar dedicated his **1941** publication, 'Thoughts on Pakistan,' to Ramabai. In the preface, Ambedkar attributes to her his evolution from an ordinary Bhiva or Bhima to Dr. Ambedkar.
Pandita Rama Bai: Pandita Ramabai was born into an upper-caste Hindu household, where her father worked as a skilled narrator of Hindu epics and mythical tales.
She wrote books such as The High Caste Hindu Woman, published in 1887. A staunch promoter of women's education and empowerment, she went on to establish the Arya Mahila Samaj, an institution committed to enhancing the status of women in India.
Ramabai's deep understanding and analyses of diverse Sanskrit texts led to her being honored with the prestigious epithet 'Saraswati', underscoring her exceptional intellectual capabilities and mastery in the field.
Annie Besant: Dr. Annie Besant took the initiative to establish two newspapers, namely Commonweal and New India. In the era of the home rule movement, she founded the National College in Madanapalle, with J.H. Cousins serving as the principal in **1916**. This institution is now recognized as Besant Theosophical College.
Notably, she attained the distinction of becoming the first female president of the Indian National Congress in **1917**. Her contributions extended to the founding of the Central Hindu College in Benaras, which was later elevated to the status of Benaras Hindu University by Pandit Madan Mohan Malaviya in **1916**.
Sarojini Naidu: A trailblazer, she holds the honor of being the first Indian woman to preside over the Indian National Congress. Her elevation to this position transpired during the Kanpur session in **1925**. Acknowledged for her remarkable prowess in poetry, she earned the title "Nightingale of India" due to her substantial contributions in the realm of poetry.
She was often referred to as 'Bharat Kokila'. Pioneering yet again, she became the inaugural woman to assume the role of governor in the dominion of India, specifically taking up the position of governor of the United Provinces in **1947**

questions.microTopic: Governor Generals and Their Policies, Administrative Developments, Judicial Developments, Civil Services, Police, Military
statementLines: "Daro-deewar pe hasrat ki nazar karte hain, khush raho ahle-vatan ham to safar karte hain." was said by:
questions.questionText: "Daro-deewar pe hasrat ki nazar karte hain, khush raho ahle-vatan ham to safar karte hain." was said by:
questions.explanationMarkdown:
**Explanation:** Option d is the correct answer. On 7th February 1856, Lord Dalhousie ordered to depose Wajid Ali Shah on the account of alleged internal misrule.
"Dar-o-Deewar par Hasrat Ki Nazar Karte Hain, Khush Raho Ahle Watan hum to safar karte hein" was composed when Wajid Ali Shah was forced to leave Oudh. The mind of Wajid Ali Shah was full of sorrow but still he felt that his country and countrymen should be well and happy.
Wajid Ali Shah himself composed Ghazals under the pen name of 'Akhtar Piya'.

questions.subject: History
questions.microTopic: Gandhian Phase (Post-1917 till Partition, including NCM, CDM, QIM, Simon Commission, etc.)
statementLines: During the Indian freedom struggle, a large unarmed crowd gathered in the Jallianwala Bagh at Amritsar on April 13, 1919 to protest against the arrest of:
questions.questionText: During the Indian freedom struggle, a large unarmed crowd gathered in the Jallianwala Bagh at Amritsar on April 13, 1919 to protest against the arrest of:

options.b: Madan Mohan Malviya and Mohammad Ali Jinnah
options.c: Mahatma Gandhi and Abul Kalam Azad
options.d: Dr. Saifuddin Kitchlu and Dr. Satyapal

questions.explanationMarkdown:
**Explanation:** Option d is the correct answer. People were gathered at Jallianwala Bagh in Amritsar to protest against the arrest of Saifuddin Kitchlew and Satyapal on 13th April **1919**. The Jallianwala Bagh massacre or the Amritsar Massacre took place when many villagers gathered in the park for the celebration of Baisakhi.
The gatherers wanted to also peacefully protest the arrest and deportation of two national leaders, Satyapal and Saifuddin Kitchlew. **Important Tips** Consequence of Jallianwala Bagh:
- On October 14, **1919**, the Government of India announced the formation of the Disorders Inquiry Committee, famously known as Hunter Committee.
- The purpose of the commission was to investigate the disturbance in Bombay, Delhi and Punjab.

questions.subject: History
statementLines: "The Congress is faltering to its fall and one of my great ambitions while in India is to assist it to a peaceful demise." This declaration was made by:
questions.questionText: "The Congress is faltering to its fall and one of my great ambitions while in India is to assist it to a peaceful demise." This declaration was made by:
questions.explanationMarkdown:
**Explanation:** Option b is the correct answer. Lord Curzon was the Viceroy of India from 1899 to **1905**. He was a critic of the Indian National Congress and he believed that it was a threat to British rule in India.
He made the statement about the Congress
- "The Congress is faltering to its fall and one of my great ambitions while in India is to assist it to a peaceful demise."
Curzon's statement was made in a private letter to his friend, Lord George Hamilton, the Secretary of State for India. The letter was leaked to the press, and it caused a great deal of anger and resentment among Indian nationalists.
Curzon's statement was also a reflection of the British government's attitude towards the Indian National Congress at the time. The British government was concerned about the growing popularity of the Congress, and it was trying to find ways to undermine its influence.

statementLines: Which of the following occurred last?
questions.questionText: Which of the following occurred last?

According to him, it was to save the public interest. **Important Tips** After the Incident of Jallianwala Bagh:
- The Bengali poet and Nobel laureate Rabindranath Tagore renounced the knighthood that he had received in **1915**.
- Gandhi soon began organizing his first large-scale and sustained nonviolent protest (satyagraha) campaign, the Non-Cooperation Movement (1920-22**).

questions.questionText: Who among the following was not present at the founding session of the Indian National Congress?
questions.explanationMarkdown:

The inaugural session of the Indian National Congress, took place in Bombay on December 28th, 1885, immediately following the conclusion of the National Conference.
Consequently, he was unable to attend the inaugural session in Bombay. **Important Tips** About Surendranath Banerjee: Basic Information:

questions.subject: History
statementLines: Hindustan Socialist Republican Association was formed by:
questions.questionText: Hindustan Socialist Republican Association was formed by:
questions.explanationMarkdown:
**Explanation:** Option c is the correct answer. After the Kakori setback, the younger revolutionaries, inspired by socialist ideas, set out to reorganise Hindustan Republic Association (HRA) at a historic meeting in the ruins of Ferozshah Kotla in Delhi in September **1928**. The name of HRA was changed to Hindustan Socialist Republican Association (HSRA) under the leadership of Chandrashekhar Azad.
The participants included Bhagat Singh, Sukhdev Thapar, Bhagwati Charan Vohra, Bejoy Kumar Sinha, Shiv Verma, and Jaidev Kapur The HSRA decided to work under a collective leadership and adopted socialism as its official goal.

questions.subject: History
statementLines: On what charge was Jatin Das arrested:
questions.questionText: On what charge was Jatin Das arrested:
questions.explanationMarkdown:
**Explanation:** Option c is the correct answer. Jatin Das arrested for his involvement in Lahore Conspiracy case of **1929**. Many young revolutionaries including Bhagat Singh, Jatin Das, Raj guru and Sukh Dev were charged for involvement in the murder of police officer J P Saunders in **1929**, which is famously known as Lahore Conspiracy Case.
Jatin Das passed away after a sixty-three-day hunger strike while he was imprisoned in the Lahore jail.

questions.questionText: Who from the following killed Michael O' Dwyer responsible for Jallianwala Bagh massacre?
questions.explanationMarkdown: **Explanation:** Option c is the correct answer. Udham Singh was a Indian revolutionary and freedom fighter belonging to the Ghadar Party. He shot and killed Michael O' Dwyer on 13 March **1940** in Britain. Michael O' Dyer was Lieutenant General of Punjab when Amritsar's Jallianwala Bagh Massacre happened on April 13, **1919**.

questions.subject: History
statementLines: Who launched the first Home Rule League Movement?
questions.questionText: Who launched the first Home Rule League Movement?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
**Explanation:** Option d is the correct answer. The Home Rule Movement was the Indian response to the First World War in a less charged but a more effective way.
Tilak started his efforts to spark a revolutionary shift by establishing a Home rule league in Maharashtra (excluding Bombay), Karnataka, and the central provinces in April **1916**. Later that year, Annie Besant founded her Home Rule movement in September **1916**.
**Important Tips** Some of the factors leading to the formation of the Home Rule Movement were as follows:
- A section of the nationalists felt that popular pressure was required to attain concessions from the government.
- The Moderates were disillusioned with the Morley-Minto reforms.
- People were feeling the burden of wartime miseries caused by high taxation and a rise in prices, and were ready to participate in any aggressive movement of protest.
- The world war I exposed the myth of white superiority.
- Tilak was ready to assume leadership after his release in June **1914**. He also admitted that the acts of violence had only served to retard the pace of political progress in India.
- Annie Besant, the Irish theosophist based in India since 1896, had decided to enlarge the sphere of her activities to include the building of a movement for home rule on the lines of the Irish Home Rule Leagues.

questions.subject: History
statementLines: Who called Bal Gangadhar Tilak the 'Father of Indian Unrest'?
questions.questionText: Who called Bal Gangadhar Tilak the 'Father of Indian Unrest'?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
**Explanation:** Option c is the correct answer. Bal Gangadhar Tilak was famously called the 'Father of Indian Unrest' by Valentine Chirol of The Times (London).
He was arrested for 18 months for writing in his newspaper criticising the measures adopted by the government to tackle the bubonic plague epidemic.
This title was used to characterize his role in the Indian freedom movement and his efforts in advocating for self-governance and challenging British rule through his nationalist activities and writings.

questions.subject: History

questions.subject: History
statementLines: Who among the following were the critics of the Swadeshi movement and pleaded for a better understanding between the East and the West?
questions.questionText: Who among the following were the critics of the Swadeshi movement and pleaded for a better understanding between the East and the West?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
**Explanation:** Option c is the correct answer. R. N. Tagore was critical of the developments during the Swadeshi movement, considering them "xenophobic, chauvinistic" Around **1908** he wrote to friend Aurobindo Mohan Bose, saying, "Patriotism cannot be our final spiritual center, I will never allow patriotism to triumph over humanity." Tagore's **1925** essay, Cult of the Charkha, severely criticised the Swadeshi movement and Gandhi's idea of 'charkha spinning' was a means to achieve independence.
"A nation," he wrote during this period, " is that aspect which a whole population assumes when organized for a mechanical purpose", a purpose often associated with a "selfishness" that "can be a grandly magnified form" of personal selfishness.
**Important Tips** Tagore's View:
- The Swadeshi movement, which gained momentum as a reaction to Lord Curzon's decision to divide Bengal along communal lines in **1905**, saw Tagore as a leading figure against the division.
- In this phase of tumult, where Tagore envisioned change would come with unity and education, he wrote songs of patriotism such as Banglar Maati Banglar Joi (Earth of Bengal, Water of Bengal) that bolstered the movements and also epitomised Hindu-Muslim unity.
- During this time, the British faced pecuniary loss and Indian industries got a chance to flourish in an environment made favourable by nationalist zeal.
- Among those who benefitted are Prafulla Chandra Ray's Bengal Chemicals, J.N. Tata's Iron and Steel Company, the Bengal Luxmi Cotton Mills and the Mohini Mill, etc.

questions.subject: History
statementLines: Which of the following pairs do NOT match?
1. British India Society - London
2. East India Association - Calcutta
3. National India Association - London
4. Indian Association - Bombay Find your answer with the help of given code:
questions.questionText: Which of the following pairs do NOT match? 1. British India Society - London 2. East India Association - Calcutta 3. National India Association - London 4. Indian Association - Bombay Find your answer with the help of given code:
questions.explanationMarkdown:
**Explanation:** Option d is the correct answer. The East India Association was not based in Calcutta but was a London-based organization. Similarly, the Indian Association was not based in Bombay but was founded in Bengal. **Important Tips** British India Society:
- The British India Society, established in 1839 in London, aimed to promote ethical practices in British India through collaboration between British and American abolitionists.
East India Company members, and Bengal's elite. Advocating against slavery, EIC misrule, and for private enterprise, it was founded by individuals like William Adam, George Thompson, William Ednis, and Major General Briggs after Adam's visit to Raja Ram Mohan Roy in India.
East India Association:
- The East India Association (EIA), founded by Dadabhai Naoroji in 1866, was a London-based organization engaging with Indian matters.
It included Indian members and retired British officials. A precursor to the Indian National Congress, the EIA later merged with other groups, evolving into the Britain, India, and Pakistan Association.
It published the Journal of the East India Association and the Asiatic Quarterly Review. National Indian Association:
- The National Indian Association (NIA), founded in 1870 by Mary Carpenter with Keshub Chunder Sen's assistance, aimed to promote female education and raise British awareness about Indian affairs.
Its London branch, established by Mrs. Manning and Elizabeth Manning in 1871, later became headquarters. The NIA facilitated social interaction between Indians and the British, organizing events, lectures, and publishing a journal.
Indian Association:
- The Indian Association, founded in 1876 by Surendranath Banerjea and Ananda Mohan Bose, emerged in Bengal as a nationalist political entity advocating local self-governance.
It replaced the Indian League and competed with the British Indian Association, mostly gaining support from Bengal's younger intelligentsia. Expanding nationwide while retaining its Bengal identity, it found provincial branches through expatriate Bengali communities.

- The Indian National Liberal Federation (INLF), founded in **1919**, emerged from a schism within the Indian National Congress.
Led by Surendra Nath Banerjea and prominent leaders like Tej Bahadur Sapru and V. S. Srinivasa Sastri, it aimed to engage in constitutional discussions with British officials.
The INLF played a role in advocating for India's constitutional representation, leading to initiatives like the Nehru Report and participation in the Round Table Conferences.
United Indian Patriotic Association:
- The United Indian Patriotic Association, founded in 1888 by Sir Syed Ahmad Khan and Raja Shiv Prasad Singh of Benaras, aimed to strengthen Muslim-British ties and counter Indian National Congress influence.
Encouraged by British officials, it opposed Congress propaganda and promoted their interests.

questions.subject: History
statementLines: Who was the leader of the Swadeshi Movement in Madras?
questions.questionText: Who was the leader of the Swadeshi Movement in Madras?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
**Explanation:** Option c is the correct answer. V. O. Chidambaram Pillai was popularly known as Kappalottiya Tamilan (The Tamil Helmsman) and Sekkizuththa Semmal (scholarly gentry who suffered at the oil press).
He entered politics in **1905** following the partition of Bengal. Towards the end of **1905**, he visited Madras and was drawn closer to the Swadeshi Movement initiated by Bal Gangadhar Tilak and Lala Lajpat Rai.
He was leader of Swadeshi movement in Madras.

questions.subject: History
statementLines: Name the first recorded Muslim who was hanged for Indian freedom-
questions.questionText: Name the first recorded Muslim who was hanged for Indian freedom-
questions.explanationMarkdown:
**Explanation:** Option c is the correct answer. Ashfaque-ullah Khan was the first recorded Muslim revolutionary who was hanged for Indian freedom. He was involved in the Kakori incident of **1925**. He met Ram Prasad Bismil in the year **1920**, and their friendship that blossomed ended only with their death in **1927**. Ashfaqullah and Bismil worked together in the Non-Cooperation Movement, campaigned for the Swaraj party, and carried out missions for the HRA, which was founded in **1924**. He wrote several poems and couplets under the pen name of 'hasrat' and 'warsi'.
Along with his fellow comrades Ramprasad Bismil and Thakur Roshan Singh, Ashfaqullah Khan was hanged on 19th December
- **1927** in Faizabad.

questions.subject: History
statementLines: Select the correct sequence of the following events by using the codes given below:
1. Jallianwala Bagh Massacre
2. Dr. Satyapal's incarceration
3. Amritsar Congress Session, 1919 Select the answer from the code given below:
Code:
questions.questionText: Select the correct sequence of the following events by using the codes given below: 1. Jallianwala Bagh Massacre 2. Dr. Satyapal's incarceration 3. Amritsar Congress Session, 1919 Select the answer from the code given below: Code:
questions.explanationMarkdown:
**Explanation:** Option a is the correct answer. The correct sequence is 2-1-3-On 10th April, **1919**, two leaders of Punjab Dr. Saifuddin Kitchlu and Dr. Satyapal were incarcerated (arrested) and deported to an undisclosed place without any trial.
It created a turmoil situation in Punjab.
- Jallianwala Bagh Massacre took place on 13 April **1919** (on Baisakhi) at Jallianwala Bagh in Amritsar, Punjab province.
This gathering was organized to oppose the "Rowlatt Act". The British officer "General Dyer" opened fire on the crowd present in the meeting, in which more than 1000 people died as reported.
Jallianwala Bagh massacre happened during the tenure of governor-general Lord Chelmsford-Indian National Congress's Amritsar session (34th Session) was held from 27 December, **1919** till 1 January, **1920** in Amritsar, Punjab.
The session was presided over by Pandit Motilal Nehru. The session took place in the aftermath of the Jallianwala Bagh massacre (April **1919**), the air-bombing of Gujranwala and the imposition of martial law in Punjab.

questions.subject: History
statementLines: Who accused Indian National Congress of practising 'politics of prayer, petition and protest'?
questions.questionText: Who accused Indian National Congress of practising 'politics of prayer, petition and protest'?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
**Explanation:** Option b is the correct answer. Bal Gangadhar Tilak accused the Indian National Congress of practising 'politics of prayer, petition and protest'.
He believed that this method was not effective in achieving independence for India. He advocated for a more militant approach, which included swadeshi (self-reliance) and swaraj (self-rule).

questions.subject: History
statementLines: Partition of Bengal was done mainly for:
questions.questionText: Partition of Bengal was done mainly for:

options.a: To divide the Hindu and Muslims
options.c: To weaken the growth of Nationalism in Bengal

questions.explanationMarkdown:
**Explanation:** Option c is the correct answer. Curzon came up with the idea to suppress the rising tide of nationalism in Bengal. According to him the Bengali intelligentsia was primarily responsible for the spread of nationalism in different parts of India.
Thus Curzon wanted to ensure that the minority consciousness should be used against the majority and that this would then break the solidarity of Indian society, and in turn, break the solidarity of the Indian National Movement as wells.
The 'Partition of Bengal' was at the core of his policies.

questions.subject: History
statementLines: Who established Indian Association in 1876 AD?
questions.questionText: Who established Indian Association in 1876 AD?

options.e: None of the above/More than one of the above

questions.explanationMarkdown:
**Explanation:** Option a is the correct answer. The Indian National Association, later known as the Indian Association of Calcutta, was established in 1876 by Surendranath Banerjee and Anand Mohan Bose.
This nationalist organization aimed to promote legitimate means for India's political, intellectual, and material advancement. It was a significant pre-Congress organization, advocating for public opinion on political matters and uniting Indians behind a common political agenda.
The association eventually merged with the Indian National Congress in 1886.

questions.subject: History
statementLines: In which place Khudiram Bose tried to kill Kingsford?
questions.questionText: In which place Khudiram Bose tried to kill Kingsford?

options.e: None of the above/More than one of the above

questions.explanationMarkdown:
**Explanation:** Option c is the correct answer. Khudiram Bose, along with Prafulla Chaki, tried to assassinate Kingsford in Muzaffarpur on the eve of 30th April **1908** AD.
Douglas H Kingsford was the Chief Presidency Magistrate of Calcutta at the time. He was a target of the revolutionaries as he was known for his harsh treatment and vindictiveness towards the freedom fighters.
**

Important Tips **Khudiram Bose:**
- Khudiram joined the Anushilan Samiti in **1908**.
- Khudiram Bose and Prafulla Kumar Chaki were appointed to carry out the mission of assassinating Kingsford.
But they failed and mistakenly assassinated the wife and daughter of a barrister called Pringle Kennedy.
- Young Khudiram, at the age of eighteen, was executed on 11 August **1908**, making him one of the youngest revolutionaries in India to be hanged by the British.
- The Poet Pitambar Das made his sacrifice immortal in the popular Bengali song Ek Baar Bidaye De Ma, a song that resonates with the passion the young boy had for his motherland.

questions.subject: History
statementLines: Who has said about Bengal Partition, "The partition announcement fell like a bomb shell"?
questions.questionText: Who has said about Bengal Partition, "The partition announcement fell like a bomb shell"?

options.e: None of the above/More than one of the above

questions.explanationMarkdown:
**Explanation:** Option a is the correct answer. 'The partition announcement fell like a bomb shell. We felt that we had been insulted, humiliated, and tricked'.
This statement was said by Surendra Nath Banerjee. **

Important Tips **Surendranath Banerjee:**
- Surendranath Banerjee was the founder of the Indian Liberation Federation (**1919**) and Indian National Association.
- He was the co-founder of the Indian National Congress.
- He was born on 10th November 1848 in Bengal and died on 6th August **1925** in Barrackpore Calcutta at the age of 76 years.
- He was influenced by the writings of Mazzini

questions.subject: History
statementLines: Which of the following was NOT objective of the Indian National Congress as pointed out by W.
C. Banerjee in 1885?
questions.questionText: Which of the following was NOT objective of the Indian National Congress as pointed out by W. C. Banerjee in 1885?

options.a: To promote contact and friendship among the protectors of the interests of the country.
options.b: To promote the feeling of nationalism among fellow countrymen by removing the feeling of casteism, communalism and regional biases.
options.c: To express the viewpoint on the necessary social issues with due consent of the educated class.
options.d: To determine on which direction and on which basis the work has to be done in the coming years for the Indian public interest.
options.e: None of the above/More than one of the above.

questions.explanationMarkdown:
**Explanation:** Option c is the correct answer. To express the viewpoint on the necessary social issues with due consent of the educated class, was **not one of the objectives** of the Indian National Congress as pointed out by W.C. Banerjee in 1885. This is because the Indian National Congress was initially formed as a moderate organization that aimed to work within the existing British system.
The moderates believed that it was important to win the consent of the British government before making any major changes to the social and political structure of India.
They also believed that it was important to involve the educated class in the freedom struggle, as they were seen as the natural leaders of the Indian people.
However, as the Indian National Congress became more radical in the early 20th century, it began to take a more active role in addressing social issues.
For example, the Congress played a major role in the passage of the Hindu Code Bill in **1956**, which gave women equal rights in marriage, divorce, and inheritance.
**

Important Tips The objectives of the Indian National Congress as pointed out by W.C. Banerjee in 1885 were:
- To promote contact and friendship among the people of all parts of India.
- To promote the feeling of nationalism among the people of India.
- To remove all racial, religious and provincial prejudices among the people of India.
- To present the views of the people of India to the government.
- To work for the improvement of the social and economic conditions of the people of India.

questions.subject: History
statementLines: Who was the Second Muslim President in the Indian National Congress?
questions.questionText: Who was the Second Muslim President in the Indian National Congress?

options.e: None of the above/More than one of the above

questions.explanationMarkdown:
**Explanation:** Option c is the correct answer. **Rahimtulla Mahomed Sayani** was the second Muslim president of the Indian National Congress.
He was a Khoja Muslim who was born in Bombay in 1847. He was a lawyer and a social reformer who was active in the Indian independence movement.
Sayani was elected president of the Indian National Congress in 1896. He was a moderate leader who believed in working within the British system to achieve self-rule for India.
He was also a strong advocate for social reform, and he worked to improve the condition of the poor and the marginalized.

questions.subject: History
statementLines: Who is the author of the popular song, Sarfaroshi Ki Tamanna Ab Hamare Dil Mein Hai?
questions.questionText: Who is the author of the popular song, Sarfaroshi Ki Tamanna Ab Hamare Dil Mein Hai?

options.e: None of the above/More than one of the above

questions.explanationMarkdown:
**Explanation:** Option e is the correct answer. **Bismil Azimabadi** is the author of the popular song, Sarfaroshi Ki Tamanna Ab Hamare Dil Mein Hai.
He was an Urdu poet from Patna, Bihar. Bismil wrote the Sarfaroshi ki Tamanna after the Jallianwala Bagh Massacre. The poem was first recited in **1920** at the Congress session of Kolkata.
In **1921**, it was published in the Urdu daily Sabah. It later became a war cry for several other freedom fighters. In **1927**, Ram Prasad Bismil chanted this poem on the way to the gallows before being hanged by the British force.
Hence, none of the above is the answer.

questions.subject: History
statementLines: Who founded Abhinav bharat society in 1906 in London?
questions.questionText: Who founded Abhinav bharat society in 1906 in London?

options.e: None of the above/More than one of the above

questions.explanationMarkdown:
**Explanation:** Option a is the correct answer. **V.D. Savarkar** founded the 'Abhinav Bharat,' a covert association established by him and his sibling Ganesh Damodar Savarkar in **1904**. Initially known as Mitra Mela and originating in Nasik, this society collaborated with numerous revolutionaries and political advocates, extending its presence to multiple locations in India as well as London.
**

Important Tips **V.D. Savarkar:** Born on May 28, 1883, in Bhagur, a village close to Nashik in Maharashtra, V.D. Savarkar embarked on an influential journey. He established the covert Abhinav Bharat Society and ventured to the United Kingdom, affiliating with groups like India House and the Free India Society.
His involvement extended to shaping the Hindu Mahasabha and holding its presidency from **1937** to **1943**. Notably, Savarkar authored 'The History of the War of Indian Independence,' which delved into the guerrilla tactics employed during the 1857 Sepoy Mutiny.
He also penned 'Hindutva: Who is Hindu,' contributing to significant ideological discussions.

questions.subject: History
statementLines: Which revolutionaries were hanged in the 'Kakori Train Robbery Case'?
questions.questionText: Which revolutionaries were hanged in the 'Kakori Train Robbery Case'?

options.c: Prafulla Chandra Chaki and Khudiram Bose
options.e: None of the above/More than one of the above

questions.explanationMarkdown:
**Explanation:** Option a is the correct answer. The Kakori Train Action involved the robbery of the Number 8 down-train travelling from Shahjahanpur to Lucknow on 9 August, **1925**. The idea was to attack the guard cabin that was carrying state money to be deposited in Lucknow.
A train was robbed at Kakori (near Lucknow) by Bismil and other members of the Hindustan Socialist Republican Army. The loot was to be used for purchasing weapons for fighting the British.
However, all the key people involved in the robbery were arrested. **Ramprasad Bismil, Ashfaqulla Khan, Rajendranath Lahiri and Roshan Singh** were sentenced to death.

questions.subject: History
questions.microTopic: Gandhian Phase (Post-1917 till Partition, including NCM, CDM, QIM, Simon Commission, etc.)
statementLines: The Jallianwala Bagh Massacre happened in the context of which Gandhian Satyagraha?
questions.questionText: The Jallianwala Bagh Massacre happened in the context of which Gandhian Satyagraha?

options.e: None of the above/More than one of the above

questions.explanationMarkdown:
**Explanation:** Option b is the correct answer. Mahatma Gandhi opposed Rowlatt's act via non-violent Satyagraha. He called a hartal on 6 April **1919**. On 13th April, Baisakhi day, a large crowd of people unaware of the prohibitory orders in the Amritsar gathered in the Jallianwala Bagh to Protest against the arrest of two congressmen Dr. Saifuddin Kitchlu and Dr. Satyal by British Officials.
The troops surrounded the gathering under orders from General Dyer and blocked the only exit point and opened fire on the unarmed crowd killing more than 1000 unarmed men, women, and children.

questions.subject: History
statementLines: Who established a branch of Anushilan Samiti at Patna in 1913?
questions.questionText: Who established a branch of Anushilan Samiti at Patna in 1913?

options.e: None of the above/More than one of the above

questions.explanationMarkdown:
**Explanation:** Option c is the correct answer. **Sachindra Nath Sanyal** established a branch of Anushilan Samiti at Patna in **1913**. Bankimchandra Mitra of BN College was given responsibilities to lead the organisation.
**

Important Tips **Anushilan Samiti:**
- The society's goal was to organise and train the youth to start a revolutionary war against the British.
- It had two branches: the Jugantar Group in Kolkata and the Dhaka Anushilan Samiti in Dhaka.
- The Samiti published "Bhavani Mandir" in **1905**, which included a thorough plan for constructing a religious sanctuary as the focal point of revolutionary operations in a remote location.
- In order to instill the required revolutionary mindset among Indians, Aurobindo Ghosh and Bipin Chandra Pal launched the Bengali nationalist weekly "Jugantar" (New Era) and its English counterpart "Bande Mataram" in March **1906**. Both publications openly preached armed revolt.
- Anushilan Samiti was a well-known covert revolutionary organisation that operated out of Bengal in the 20th century with the goal of removing colonial authority and igniting India's independence movement.

questions.subject: History
statementLines: Who was the President of Gaya Session of the Indian National Congress held in 1922?
questions.questionText: Who was the President of Gaya Session of the Indian National Congress held in 1922?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
**Explanation:** Option a is the correct answer. **Chittaranjan Das** was the President of the Gaya Session of the Indian National Congress held in **1922**. He was a prominent Indian nationalist and lawyer who was a close associate of Mahatma Gandhi.
He was a strong advocate of the Non-Cooperation Movement and he played a key role in its success. **

Important Tips The Gaya Session of the Indian National Congress was a significant event in the history of the Indian independence movement.
It was at this session that the Congress adopted the Swaraj Constitution, which was a blueprint for self-government in India. The session also saw the Congress declare its policy of non-violence and its commitment to Hindu-Muslim unity.

questions.subject: History
statementLines: Which movement started after the Partition of Bengal?
questions.questionText: Which movement started after the Partition of Bengal?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
**Explanation:** Option b is the correct answer. **Swadeshi Movement** was started in **1905** to oppose the British decision of partitioning of Bengal.
It involved boycotting British products and the revival of domestic products and production processes. The objective was to exert sufficient pressure on the Government to prevent the unjust partition of Bengal from being implemented.
But still the government went on with the decision and announced the partition in July **1905**. **

Important Tips Events and their occurrence years:
- Civil Disobedience Movement-March 12, **1930**.
- Swadeshi Movement-August 7, **1905**.
- Quit India Movement-August 8, **1942**.
- Non-Cooperation Movement-August 1, **1920**.

questions.subject: History
statementLines: The protest method of moderate leaders of the Congress was:
questions.questionText: The protest method of moderate leaders of the Congress was:
questions.explanationMarkdown:
**Explanation:** Option b is the correct answer. The protest method of the moderate leaders of the Congress was **constitutional agitation**.
They believed that the best way to achieve self-rule for India was through persuasion and cooperation with the British. They used methods such as petitions, protests, and delegations to the British government to voice their demands. **

Important Tips The Moderates, in pursuit of their objectives, employed a range of methods:
- Demand for Reforms and Critique: They articulated demands for reform and critiqued government policies, aiming to improve Indian conditions.
- Preference for Non-Violence: Instead of violence, they advocated patience and reconciliation, focusing on peaceful means to achieve their goals.
- Constitutional and Peaceful Approaches: They relied on constitutional methods and peaceful strategies, seeking change through legal and established channels.
- Educational Initiatives: Their efforts centered on educating and awakening public political awareness, fostering informed opinions.
- Creating British Public Opinion: To garner support in England, they organized lectures and published a weekly journal named 'India' for British readership.
- Newspapers and Journals: They utilized newspapers like the Bengali press, Bombay Chronicle, Hindustan Times, Induprakash, Rast Goftar, and the weekly journal 'India' to criticize government policies.
- Public Meetings and Discussions: They convened meetings across locations like England, Mumbai, Allahabad, Pune, and Calcutta, addressing social, economic, and cultural issues.
- Memoranda and Petitions: The Moderates drafted and submitted memoranda and petitions to both the Indian and British officials, along with the British Parliament.
These documents aimed to enlighten the British public and leaders about Indian conditions, fostering understanding and support for change.

questions.subject: History
statementLines: Who said that "Congress Movement was neither inspired by the people, nor devised or planned by them"?
questions.questionText: Who said that "Congress Movement was neither inspired by the people, nor devised or planned by them"?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
**Explanation:** Option d is the correct answer. **Lala Lajpat Rai** said "Congress Movement was neither inspired by the people, nor devised or planned by them" because he believed that the Congress Movement was dominated by a small group of elite Indians who were out of touch with the needs of the masses.
He also believed that the Congress Movement was too focused on achieving self-rule through constitutional means and that it was not doing enough to address the social and economic problems facing India.

questions.subject: History
statementLines: Who propounded the doctrine of 'Passive Resistance'?
questions.questionText: Who propounded the doctrine of 'Passive Resistance'?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
**Explanation:** Option d is the correct answer. The **Doctrine of Passive Resistance** proposed by **Aurobindo Ghose** (1872
- **1950**).
It was an alternative to expelling the colonial power from the country. He calls for organized political resistance to achieve liberty for India.
For this goal, he proposes the concept of "Passive Resistance", with the connected ideas of self-development and self-help. According to Aurobindo, through this concept, Indians could effectively dispute every facet of colonial rule, without engaging in armed rebellion.
A four-tier program was suggested for this; development of India as a self-governing country, evolving the defense capability, driving out the foreign rule from every sphere of society and establishing self-rule.

questions.subject: History

The session was presided over by **Dadabhai Naoroji**.

questions.subject: History
statementLines: Which one of the following leaders belonged to the Extremist wing of the Congress?
questions.questionText: Which one of the following leaders belonged to the Extremist wing of the Congress?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
**Explanation:** Option a is the correct answer. **Aurobindo Ghosh**, born in 1872, was a key figure in the Extremist wing of the Indian National Congress.
Advocating for Indias freedom, he led revolutionary efforts through youth clubs like Anushilan Samiti. As principal of Kolkata's National College, Ghosh championed radical means for independence.
His role in the **1907** Congress split between Moderates and Extremists underscored his stance. Arrested in the **1908** Alipore Bomb Case, Ghosh's resolute commitment to India's liberation remains notable in the nation's history.

questions.subject: History
statementLines: Who gave the 'Inquilab Zindabad' slogan?
questions.questionText: Who gave the 'Inquilab Zindabad' slogan?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
**Explanation:** Option c is the correct answer. Inquilab Zindabad, can be translated as "Long Live Revolution" was coined by Moulana Hasrat Mohani in the year **1921**. It was one of the most famous slogans during the Indian freedom struggle.
It was popularized by **Bhagat Singh** during the late 1920s through his speeches and writings. This slogan was shouted by Bhagat Singh and his companion B. K. Dutt after bombing the Central Legislative Assembly in **1929**.

questions.subject: History
statementLines: The founder of the Indian National Congress was a:
questions.questionText: The founder of the Indian National Congress was a:
questions.explanationMarkdown:
**Explanation:** Option a is the correct answer. The founder of the Indian National Congress was Allan Octavian Hume, a retired British Indian **Civil Service (ICS) officer**.
He was a social reformer and a political activist who was concerned about the lack of political representation for Indians under British rule.
He founded the Indian National Congress in 1885 as a forum for educated Indians to discuss political and social issues.

questions.subject: History

questions.questionText: Most of the moderate leader hailed from:
questions.explanationMarkdown:

They believed that the best way to achieve self-rule for India was through persuasion and cooperation with the British. They believed that the British could be persuaded to give India self-rule if the Indian people were united and presented a strong case.
Some of the prominent moderate leaders included Dadabhai Naoroji, Gopal Krishna Gokhale, Surendranath Banerjea, and Pherozeshah Mehta. They were all from urban areas and had a good education.

Lord Curzon served as the Governor General and Viceroy of India from 6 January 1899 to 18 November **1905**.

questions.subject: History
statementLines: Home Rule Movement was symbol of starting a new phase of freedom movement in India because:
questions.questionText: Home Rule Movement was symbol of starting a new phase of freedom movement in India because:

options.a: It put a perfect project for the self-Government before the nation
options.b: Leadership of the movement came in the hands of Gandhiji
options.c: Hindus and Muslims started to struggle jointly
options.d: It established co-ordination between extremists and moderates

questions.explanationMarkdown:
**Explanation:** Option a is the correct answer. Home Rule Movement was symbol of starting a new phase of freedom movement in India because **it put a perfect project for the self-Government before the nation**.
They put forth demand of self-government or home rule for all of India within the British commonwealth. They clearly mentioned their ultimate objective. **

Important Tips Activities and programmes conducted during Home Rule Movement:
- B.G. Tilak through Home Rule put his demands before British government that included swarajya, formation of linguistic states and education in the vernacular.
- The Movement attracted the hitherto 'politically backward' regions of Gujarat and Sindh.
- Activities and programmes conducted during movements were-promoting political education and discussion through public meetings, organising libraries and reading rooms containing books on national politics, holding conferences, and organising classes for students on politics.
- The Programmes also carried out propaganda through newspapers, pamphlets, posters, illustrated post-cards, plays, religious songs, etc.
- The activities also included collecting funds, organising social work, and participating in local government activities.

These members got together and decided that it was necessary to have a national alliance that would work throughout the year (unlike the Congress which had annual sessions).

questions.subject: History
statementLines: What was the cause of the outbreak of Ghadar Revolution?
questions.questionText: What was the cause of the outbreak of Ghadar Revolution?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
**Explanation:** Option b is the correct answer. The Komagata Maru was a Japanese steamship that was chartered by Gurdit Singh, a Sikh businessman from Punjab, India.
Singh had organized the voyage to Canada in order to transport Indian immigrants who were seeking a better life. The Komagata Maru arrived in Vancouver, British Columbia, on May 23, **1914**. The Canadian government denied the immigrants entry, claiming that they did not have the necessary paperwork.
The immigrants were forced to stay on board the ship for two months, while the Canadian government debated their fate. On July 23, **1914**, the Komagata Maru was forced to leave Vancouver and return to India.
The return journey was also difficult, and the ship was attacked by a British warship. The **Komagata Maru incident** galvanized support for the Ghadar Party, a revolutionary organization that was committed to overthrowing British rule in India.
The Ghadar Party launched a series of armed uprisings in India in **1915**, but they were all unsuccessful.

questions.subject: History
statementLines: Who called the Congress Conference as the 'Annual National Festival of Educated Indians'?
questions.questionText: Who called the Congress Conference as the 'Annual National Festival of Educated Indians'?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
**Explanation:** Option a is the correct answer. **Lala Lajpat Rai** was a Punjabi lawyer, politician, and freedom fighter. He was also a critic of the moderate leadership of the Congress, which he believed was too willing to compromise with the British.
In **1907**, Rai described the Congress Conference as the "Annual National Festival of Educated Indians". He argued that the Congress was a gathering of the elite, and that it did not represent the interests of the masses. He also criticized the Congress for its focus on constitutional methods, arguing that these methods were not effective in achieving self-government.

Important Tips Abul Kalam Azad Azad was a prominent Indian scholar, educationist, and freedom fighter.
He was a close associate of Mahatma Gandhi and he was a leading figure in the Non-Cooperation Movement and the Quit India Movement. Azad was a firm believer in secularism and he worked to promote religious harmony in India. He was also a strong advocate of education and he played a key role in the development of the Aligarh Muslim University.

options.a: Separate electorate demand of Muslim League was accepted.
options.b: Temporary merger of Muslim League and Congress occurred
options.c: A Muslim person was elected as President of Congress

questions.subject: History
statementLines: Which of the following revolutionaries was not hanged for Lahore Conspiracy?
questions.questionText: Which of the following revolutionaries was not hanged for Lahore Conspiracy?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
**Explanation:** Option a is the correct answer. **Batukeshwar Dutt** was not hanged for Lahore Conspiracy. He was caught for throwing bomb in the Central Legislative Assembly on April 8, **1929** and sentenced to life imprisonment.
**

Important Tips **Batukeshwar Dutt:**
- He graduated from Prithvinath College, Kanpur. In his college days, he came in contact with Shahid Bhagat Singh and was inspired by him to join the Hindustan Socialist Republican Association.
- He was a member of the "Naujawan Bharat Sabha" founded by Bhagat Singh in March **1926**.
- After throwing the bombs in the Central Legislative Assembly on April 8, **1929**, the iconic duo was arrested on the same day.
- He continued his fight for human rights even in prison.
- He joined Bhagat Singh in one of the longest ever hunger strikes, running up to 114 days, to support their demand for a better living standard for the prisoners.
- He was sentenced to life imprisonment and was sent to the Andaman Cellular Jail. Dutta was later shifted to Hazaribag jail, Delhi jail and Patna jail.
- He was released from Patna jail in **1938**. He was suffering from several ailments including tuberculosis. Despite his frail health, he joined Gandhiji in the Quit India movement in **1942**. He was again arrested and imprisoned for another four years.
- He succumbed to cancer at AIIMS, Delhi on 20th July **1965**.
- Honouring his last will, he was cremated at Hussainiwala, where Bhagat Singh, Rajguru, and Sukhdev were laid to rest.
